"Yes. father." returned the young stu
dent simply. He knew the danger that
threatened the mission of Hoi Mun and
all its people. He knew that there was
the mission of "The Open Door" a dark,
stealthy form slid through the swaying

Felix and the coolie servant traveled
destination, they stopped for rest. With
the messages safe in his breast Felix
crept in beside a bank, and with his head
upon h/s pack fell asleep, while the coolie
stones and missiles. Felix felt a mad,

but as the utter uselessness of all effort

• • •
Waking, he opened his eyes to the cool

escort of sufficient number to lnstrre re

ariother. It was the faithful Wong Kai.
ERNESTINE COUGHRAN.
THE presence of a great unrest was
felt in the Valley of Ho Tal. Up on
the hillsid* the mission of the Jesuit
Fathers wa? as tranquil and as dig
nified as at all times, but its silence was
the quiet of apprehension.
Presently at on© of the windows there
ttm a stir. It was the window of Father
Honore'fi library and the curtain had
been drawn by a hand.
"Sit down, my *on," paid the good
father. "The air Is stifling. You are sure
it Is your wish to go?"
fast. Far down the edge of the canal,
past fantastic bridges, gaudy pa
godas and the " grotesque temples
of tho , .neighboring villages. then
out into the illimitable plain, with
its network of canals, where .the
stretches of brown fallow showed a
dreary, clean-cut horizon, unbroken by
tree or hedge-row. Toward the Vising- of
the sun they slept, and. when it was light
they breakfasted and started on. ' Jour
neying en . they * passed the sail-carts of
the rice' farms, with' canvas spread and
driven by the s wind. Here and there were
barges riding on wheels through the ca
nals. Th»t^was all-ran endless waste of
the . same, along the treeless level. At
first there v.'as no trouble and they passed
unmolested through the groups of boat
men and villagers, simple folks unaware
that their; country was at war with the
allied powers .of the world. But a day
and a night brought them to a place of
scowling faces and ominous mutterings.
It t was when. the>v turned from the great
waterway and struck . across .the-, plain
toward Shanghai.. At every village, the
scowls grew blacker, and the coolie crept
silently at the heels of his master, ner
vous and v/atchfu'l. Being refused a
lodging Felix grew, afraid to ask and they
took turns at sleeping In the open fields,
or In the cooler nights under some friend
ly bank. They skirted the towns and
avoided inhabited places.
One 'night, 'but forty miles from his
¦ * '
peril in the . air and that at any moment
might come the outbreak of the rebellious
element, which meant a horrible and re
volting death for all the Christians- in the
settlement of the valley of Good-to-look
at. To him had been intrusted the mess
age for the commander of the French
squadron at Shanghai and through him
was to come the assistance which was to
save the # mission if it were possible. He
knew that his was no easy task; that the
way lay through a country wild with riot
ing" and nervous with the breath of hor
rible crimes. He knew that there .was
little hope of threading a safe .path
through the perils' of the section through
which he would have to pass, and- that
the uncertainty of life carried with it
the horrors of a -death unspeakable. -But
Felix Marchand was 22 and no coward.
As the last of the red sun sunk outof
eight behind the rice fields Felix set out,
close at his heels the servant of the mis
sion, Chung Foo, with a bag of provisions
on his back. At the hridge where the
' canal was widest they met a tall, serious
yellow man. It was Wong Kai. With a
word and a noJ Felix passed him by, but
the slant eyes watched long after the
careless good-by. /
. "Me sabe he go." mused Wong,
-"Mishee FJe>»£~ go Shanghai. "\3~ong.Kal
', go too. Mishee Flelix goo' man. No likee
see go dead." Then when the sun. was
• not ct all and the dark had settled 'over
with tireless ' eyes watched beside him.
Presently there were wild sounds off
toward the dark line of horizon . and
Chung Foo sprang to hif feet. Physical
exhaustion >had rendered' Felix insensible
to the danger of abandonment "and he
slerJt soundly. The words ,of the coolie
reached his ears but not hte senses, as
Chung Foo hastily muttered in ; pigeon
English: "You stay; mo go look- see. No
come back you run likee devil. Big sword
man come, me sabe alle day, makee fol
low."
The little yellow man darted off, glid
ing through the grasses, and Felix slept
on. Roused/ aften how long a time he
knew not. by the most fiendish succession
of heathen yells ever given vent to-out
side the limits of the infernal regions,
Felix leaped to his feet, instantly awake
to the perils of his position, and found
Chung Foo missing. The momentary sus
picion of treachery against his faithful
servant came x back to him as a bitter re
proach a few minutes later, when the
real situation unfolded its horrors to his
realization. The. Boxers had laid hold.of
the devoted coolie and were torturing him
with the fury of disappointment. They
had sought his master. , Peering from the
embankment, Felix beheld a sickening
sight. By. the light of torches, a dozen
howling fiends circled around the body of
poor Chung Foo, lashing him with whips
and prodding him with sticks, burning
him with ' flaming torches and throwing
to assist the dying coolie appeared to him,
he hastily picked up the lighter pack up
on which he had slept, and cautiously
moved across the waste toward a strag
gling field of corn where he hoped to gain
a shelter for the ' hour^of approaching
daylight. Heartsick,' weary and footsore
he dragged himself into the scant thick
et of- low growth just as the straggling
beams of the sun crept over the desolate
waste and lighted up the land of his foes.
Breathless and with a whirling head he
lay like a hunted animal close to the
ground, eachwhisk of. the -wind through
the drying leaves of corn bringing a-mo
ment of agonized anticipation. NerveSj
overwrought, and utterly exhausted phys
ically. Felix fell asleep and slept deeply.
"The red sun faded into, pale yellow, and
then beat down a white heat. Felix
awoke feeling cramped. He attempted to
rise, but found he could not. His hand3
were bound. His dazed brain began to
comprehend. He had been thus bound
and left to die of starvation. There was
a discordant sound beside him. Felix
heard it and "understood starvation was
not to be the only torture. It was to be
the terrible punishment v of- the parrot, of
which he had. heard. Hands and feet.se
curely bound.'he couid not move, and to
his belt was chained a monster green bird.
The fiends had sought him out while ho
slept, and had done this thing .while he
lay senseless in* the stupor of exhaustion.
Great beads i of perspiration came out
on Felix's brow.- His face was white and
drawn. Presently he would grow hungry,
and the bird— would eat. In the horror
of it* all, withi the pitiless white, rays of
the sun beating down in his face, Felix
cried out,', one f agonized, despairing cry
that lost itself on the shimmering hot air.
Then he lay still.' The bird hopped upon
his breast, cocked one eye and looked
around curiously, then it settled the gaze
of two round waiting eyes upon Felix's
face, and-it too, was still. The lagging
minutes crept; into hours, the two round
eyes still watched and waited. It^» could
not be much longer, then the sharp beak
and the black' claws would be at work;
theTway the parrot gazed with those
round, red-rimmed eyes into his own told
Felix what would be the first work of/the
sharp" beak.-. In the horror of it all he
nightmare had been with him while ho
slept, and he had not forgotten, "^e
thought for the moment he had gone nyw;
and expected to hear the discordant cry
of the bird, or feel It pluck at ht3 shirt.
He opened his eye3 again, and then he
tried to move his hands. To hia surprise
he succeeded, though his whole body was
aching, and stiff. Sinking back, ho tried
to think it all out. and gave it up. H a
felt his brain going, then there Was a feel
ing of something at his wrist, a hand was
rubbing it. Slowly he gathered his senses
and looked up. A dark form carao be
tween him and the stars.
"Whashe mallah? Mishee Flelix ffeel
blettah?" It was Wong Kai— Wons Kai
who had followed through the days and
nights. . sleepless and faithful, until ha
had lost them two days before, and even
now he had traced Felix through the
trails of his pursuers, and past the muti
lated body of Chung Foo to the patch of
corn, and he had lain low until the last
sound of the rabble had died away.
All day they waited in the corn, resting
and sleeping. Wong Kai had brought
with him food, and they ate. To all Felix's
heartfelt words of gratitude the stoio
Wong Kal made no reply, except that ha
had followed of his own free- will, because
he "no likee see Mlshee Flelix go dead."
But when Felix made him lie down to
sleep, and bound a wet handkerchief over
the yellow, aching forehead there was <^«»
beaming, peaceful look of gratified amf.
tion from Wong's* flat features, and th«
yellow man slept.
They had moved through to the far end
of the cornfield, leaving no trace of their
footsteps, lest the fiends should, coma
again to witness the agonies of tbfelr vic
tim. But they saw no trace of. them, and
when it was dusk they started forward.
They traveled fast, being refreshed with
their long day of rest, and the morning
sunshine found them within sight of th«
city of Shanghai. At noontime as th»
commander of the French squadron
lunched at his table a message cams to
him. It was to the effect that a messen
ger from the Jesuit Mission of Hot Mun,
in the Valley of Ho Tal, awaited an audi
ence with the admiral, and brought let
ters of importance. The admiral's lunch
eon was not finished, for with tho coming
of Felix there was much to do.
The following day Felix set out upon hia
return, but he was not alone. A French

President McKlnley's Policy.
In' his welcoming speech to the returned
Per v-vlvanta troops the president clearly pre-
Per ylvi
fits
sfu . his policy towards tne rniiippines. lie
says: "They assailed our sovereignty and there
will be no useless parley no pause until the
insurrection is suppressed and American
authority acknowledged and established."
His justification ot this war policy of subjuga
tion is based upon these words: "Peace
brought to us the Philippines, by treaty cession
fro.n Spain. . . It became our territory and, is
ours, as much as the Louisiana purchase or
Texas or Alaska."
As one of the sovereign people the writer did
not understand that we were still in theslaveiy

gether with the islands which they inhabited.
r . 1 . 1 1 1. . 1 1 3 . 1 .

humane mission, and out of real goodness paid
Spain $20,000,000 to relinquish all claims on
Cuba and the Philippines, that we might
give the people such liberty and such a repre
sentative government as we ourselves enjoy.
These people were struggling tor liberty and
a representative government, they desired a
voice as to what principles should govern them,
and the writer supposed that we were going to
give them the rights for which they had been
fighting to obtain, and help them to establish
th? best possible government. But this is not

o ce Spain's, and they are now ours. Spain

inos. Nor had our forefathers any more rijht

An Unfair Competition.
From Orange Judd Farmer.
The alien contract labor law is- being griev
ously violated in Hawaii. No specific form of
government having been provided by congress
lor the Sandwich Islands, the Dole regime con
tinues in power. It is notoriously controlled
bv the sugar planters, who are developing their
plantations on a most extraordinary scale by
the use of artificial irrigation and Japanese and
Chinese coolie labor. Nearly $20,000,000 has
been invested in plantation improvements there
since annexation, and more than 12,000 coolies
have been imported during the past few
mouths. These Asiatic coolies, who are vir
tually slaves, now ontnuinbei the rest of the
population, both native and foreign.
No check whatever has yet been placed on
the importation of this contract labor, nor h is
anything been done to curb the slave system
under which this labor is worked on planta
tions. Indeed, the situation is even worse
than Orange Judd Farmer predicted, before
annexation was decided upon, that it would be
come. The talk about prohibiting coolie im
migration, about encouraging white labor, etc.,
etc., proves to have been mere buncombe, as
we showed it to te at the time.
The island planters hope soon to increase
the production of sugar from 250,000 tons to
500,000 tons, all of which claims free access to
the markets ot tlie United States. The sugar
made with coolie or slave labor can, of course,
undersell that produced by the beet growers of
of the north and west, or the cane growers of
our own southern states, and anything that
tends to restrict the beet acreage, by so much
increases the area of other crops, aud thus in
directly reduces the value of all farm produce.
The question therefore is, Shall our American
fanners and tax-payers be Obliged to compete
with the coolie labor of Hawaii?
A few years ago the tobacco growers of New
York and Wisconsin made a great protest be
cause they were indirectly forced to compete
with coolie labor in raising tobacco on the
island of Sumatra. And we all know what a
tremendous howl goes up from the lahor unions
of the country whenever a few contract labor
ers are imported from other countries in defi
ance of the federal law. But here is the whole
territory of Hawaii allowed not only to main
tain a coolie system of labor, but to increase it
until the coolies comprise the major portion of
its population. If these Hawaiian slaves were
competing with any branch of labor repre
sented in our American labor unions, these or
ganizations would long ago have made a pro
test against it. The labor unions would have
shaken the country from centertocircuinference,
and congress would have legislated in hot haste
against the evil under penalty of being turned
out at the next election by vote of the labor
unions. But our American fanners and their
hiieu men do not yet recognize the extent of
this unfair competition .and are not organized
to prevent it as aie the labor unions.
Vet they have got to wake up to their inter
ests or the coolie slave system will be extended
from Hawaii to the Philippines, Cuba and
Porto Rico, in whose behalf the sugar and to
bacco trusts are already clamoring loudly for
free admission of tropical produce into the
United States, inerican farmers will soon
realize the extent of this danger. And once
they are aroused, congress must correct the
evil or there would be a new deal at Washing
ton. Notice to this effect is hereby served
upon the politicians of all parties.
More than a vear ago we showed that, in its
economic aspects, the results of the Spanish
war would hs to raise just this new agricultural
issue th'at is now looming up. Many states
men and journals- who then accused us of pes
siin'sm now realize the truth of our position.
This question will not down, but is bound to
gro.v in intensity. As between the sugar trust
and coolie labor on the one hand, aud the in
terests of our American farmers on the other,
who can doubt the final result? Nor will state
or national bounties or any other makeshifts
serve to compromise the situation. Michigan
now lefuses to pay the bounty her legislature
voted, and a national bounty is hardly tc be
thought of. The farmers of the United States
have carried their share of "protection" these
many years, with results that have always been
questioned. But now here is a chance where
protection will really protect the farmer and
laborer engaged in growing sugar beets or
sugar cane within these continental United
States. If te fanner is now to be sacrificed at
the behest of the sugar refiners' trust, he knows
how to strike back.
jslaiids have been purchased, and without the
consent of the people.
Besides, there Is no parallel between the ad
mission of Louisiana, Texas and Alaska.
Colonists 111 Texas established a republic,
which was acknowledged by the United .States.
Twice that republic sought, and was refused,
admittance to the Union. After the republic
existed ten years and the republic had in
creased to 150,000, the United States sought and
seemed the annexation of Texas in 1845. It
mat'ers not that the settlement of the boundary
line involved the United States in war with
Mexico. The Texaus not only rebelled against
Mexico, but desired union, while the Filip
inos rebelled against Spain, and the United
States is pursuing the policy of conquest to
make the Filipinos consent to union. Nor
was the purchase of Louisina followed
with resistance. The battle following was
with Great Britain. So far as Alaska is con
cerned, the Indians had not been seeking to
establish a representative and independent
government, and we had no need to enter upon
a war of conquest. As Americans and citizens
of this republic, we have no right to expansion
through conquest. Our expansion should be
qnly by the consent of the inhabtauts. Nor
has the Lord given us a right to expand in any
other way. As a nation we are now trampling
upon those sacred rights that God has vouch
safed to every min, and which we prize so
highly ourselves. We are now laying our
selves liable to the same judgment that rested
upon oppressive Spain. While we were break
ing the tyrannizing power of Spain and setting
the oppressed fret, God's signal blessing was
upon us; but since our war began against the
oppressed, we see no evidence of special divine
favor. Let us beware. If we love our land,
let us not, by injustice, bring God's curse.
Correspondent.
Would Not Eat "Embalmed" Beef.
Honolulu, Aug. 26 (via San Francisco,
Sept. 2.) S. M. T. Four clays ago the United
States transport Senator arrived here en route to
Manila. Two days later a number of soldiers
bought several bottles ot methylated spirits of
alcohol, and were soon half insane under its in
fluence. A general fight ensued, which soon
involved over a hundred soldiers. A riot call
was sounded, and the police arrested the ring
leaders. On the way to the station the soldiers'
friends attacked the police, but citizens and the
prevost guard came to their aid, A few hours
later the Senator sailed with many soldiers in
irons,
The second day out from .San Francisco the
soldiers on the Senator discovered that a con
siderable of the beef aboard for their consump
tion was the tinned beef, which has acquired
the name of "embalmed beet." Trouble arose
immediately. The soldiers refused to eat it or
allow it to be served. They insisted that it be
thrown overboard, aud finally this was done.
Criticism Critisized.
We quote the following from the Midland,
one of the organs of the United Presbyterian
church. The article was written by J.
McCulloch:
In the issue of the Midland of July aand, the
editor criticised J. M. "Foster's article for inti
mating that the church should call a member
to account for wrong doing in the capacity of a
public servant, calling such action a dangerous
ecclesiasticism. Now the question resolves it
self into this: Cau a man in public life do a
wrong with impunity that would bring him
under the discipline of the church if done in
private life?
"When Governor Drake, of Iowa, signed a
bill authorizing the manufacture of intoxicat
ing liquors, while another law in the same
state forbade it, the Standard, of Cincinnati, the
leading organ of the church of which Mr.
Drake was a member, advised the church at
Centerville, where he held his membership, to
turn him out, and I think the advice was cor
rect. It was a diabolical act and a moral out
rage on the good people of the state.
"So when President McKinley upholds his
subordinate in defying the congress of the
United States by refusing to execute the anti
canteen law, thus causing demoralization, dis
ease and death in our army, I think the Meth
odist church at Canton, Ohio, or in Washing
ton, if his membership has been transferred,
lus a right to discipline him with a view to his
reformation. When he drinks intoxicating
liquors in his pubiic life and sets them before .
his guests at his table, he is just as liable to
censure by his church as though he did it at
his home in Canton.
"The church may not have the right to dic
tate to a public officer what he shall do,, but.
when he violates the law ot the church in his
public capacity and brings reproach on the
cause of Christ and the Christianity of the
nation, it is certainly the duty of the church to
call him to account for it.
"I have no sympathy with the doctrine that
a man can do in a public capacity what, his
conscience forbids him in his private life. Ii
a judge on the bench believes the licensing of
the liquor traffic is wrong, he violates his con
science when he issues a license on the petition
of a number of cititens, notwithstanding the
law says he may. The study of the lessons
in Daniel of late teaches that such an officer
must refuse to do the wrong and take the con
sequences or resign his position because of its
requirements; and if we had a few public offic
ers who had the stamina to do so, the eyes
of a sleeping church would soon be opened to
the sin of fellowshiping a 'throne of iniquity
which ftameth mischietby law.' "
Tine men who create the wealth of the world
earn it, but do not obtain it; wealth, under the
competitive system, is appropriated by the few
who earn nothing. Flaming Sword.

A PASS TO PEKIN
• By Vincent Sheean
MISS MARY WAPLES of
Newton Center, Massa
chusetts, came out of
the Mukden hotel and
glanced timidly about her. The
streets of Boston and New York
bad always filled her with de
licious terror; what, then, was
she to make of the streets of
Mukden, so lately a battleground
between these mysterious and
quarrelsome Orientals? The spir
it of Miss Mary Waples positively
curled up with fright as her eye
fell upon the Japanese sentry on
duty at the entrance to her hotel.
He was a businesslike little fel
low with solemn face and shiny
bayonet.
"A ricksha,” said Miss Waples
flutteringiy.
It really didn’t make much
difference which ricksha she
took. There they were, a dozen
or more, and nothing much to
choose between them: each little
cart with ns human beast at
tached, ragged and miserable aud
dejected.
The coolie who took the live
liest interest was the raggedest
and aorriest-looking of the herd.
Hour after hour he had been
standing in front of the hotel,
turning his sad eyes toward the
North; up there the smoke still
curled above the ruins of the
Chinese array barracks. The Bor
riest-looking ricksha coolie, we
may as well tell you at once, was
named Ku Ta-mu; and if those
syllables mean nothing to you,
what is the use of reading the
newspapers? Ku Ta-mu was a
war-lord; Ku Ta-inu had figured
on the front pages of the world’s
newspapers for a whole week
past, and on their less distin
guished pages for years. At the
present moment, as Miss Waples
emerged from the Mukden hotel
and fluttered her timid request
for a ricksha, Ku Ta-mu was be
ing diligently sought for by
Japanese troops throughout the
city and district of Mukden.
It need scarcely surprise us,
therefore, to see Ku Ta-mu look
ing dejected as he waited in front
of the hotel. Beyond the smoke
of the barracks which had once
housed his troops lay his great
palace, with his five silken
sheathed concubines, his three
thousand pounds of opium, his
fifty chests crammed with Ming
and Manchu treasures, and his
vaults filled with three million
dollars in Shanghai gold bars. It
was extremely probable that at
this instant soldiers were divid
ing these treasures of gold and
flesh and smoke and jade among
them. And General Ku Ta-mu
himself,—a sovereign,.barely two
days before, — owed his relative
freedom and his thirty-four-year
old head to this humiliating dis
guise as a ricksha man. Sooner
or later — probably sooner — he
was bound to be recognized by
some Japanese spy. And then
he would unquestionably lose his
Shanghai gold bars, to say noth
ing of his head.
THESE melancholy reflections
were not lightened by any
hope of escape; for. indeed, what
possibility of escape was there?
Even to get to the railway sta

in gold when the diplomats put
the claim through.
“Take your place* in the
train.” said the war-lord shortly,
“and thank your friend Miss
Waples that you are able to do
so. She may tell you why.”
Nowadays, of course. Miss
Waples is frequently obliged to
tell the story of her extraordin
ary journey across Manchuria,
for she is a heroine to all Peip
ing. Including the Winterboth
ams. She is a great deal more
knowing about it now than she
was at the time.
"Of course I can’t say that I
recognized him in his disguise.”
she says romantically hut honest
ly. “And still, there was a
marked difference—he wasn’t in
the least like the other coolies—
and one can tell such things at
a glance, don’t you think?”

title: Brownsville herald. [volume]

text:
A PASS TO PEKIN
MISS MARY WAPLES of
Newton Center, Massa
chusetts, came out of
the Mukden hotel and
glanced timidly about her. The
streets of Boston and New York
had always filled her with de
licious terror; what, then, was
she to make of the streets of
Mukden, so lately a battleground
between these mysterious and
quarrelsome Orientals? The spir
it of Miss Mary Waples positively
curled up with fright as her eye
fell upon the Japanese sentry on
duty at the entrance to her hotel.
He was a businesslike little fel
low with solemn face and shiny
bayonet.
**A ricksha,” said Miss Waples
flutteringly.
It really didn't make much
difference which ricksha she
took. There they were, a dozen
or more, and uothiug much to
choose between them: each little
cart with its human beast at.
tached, ragged and miserable and
dejected.
The coolie who took the live
liest Interest was the raggedest
and sorrlest-looklng of the herd.
Hour after hour he had been
standing in front of the hotel,
turning his sad eyes toward the
North; up there the smoke still
curled above the ruins of the
Chinese an ly barracks. The sor
riest-looking ricksha coolie, we
may as well tell you at once, was
named Ku Ta-mu; and if those
syllables mean nothing to you.
what is the use of reading the
newspapers? Ku Ta-mu was a
war-lord; Ku Ta-mu had figured
on the front pages of the world’s
newspapers for a whole week
past, and on their less distin
guished pages for years. At the
present moment, as Miss Waples
emerged from the Mukden hotel
and fluttered her timid request
for a ricksha, Ku Ta-mu was be
ing diligently sought for by
Japanese troops throughout the
city and district of Mukden.
ii neea scarcely surprise us,
therefore, to see Ku Ta-mu look
ing dejected as lie waited in front
of the hotel. Beyond the smoke
of the barracks which had once
housed his troops lay his great
palace, with his live silken
sheathed concubines, his three
thousand pounds of opium, his
fifty chests crammed with Ming
and Matichu treasures, and his
vaults filled with three million
dollars in Shanghai gold bars. It
was extremely probable that at
this instant soldiers were divid
ing these treasures of gold and
flesh and smoke and jade among
them. And General Ku Ta-mu
himself,—a sovereign, barely two
days before. — owed his relative
freedom and his thirty-four-year
old head to this humiliating dis
guise as a ricksha man. Sooner
or later — probably sooner — he
was bound to be recognized by
some Japanese spy. And then
he would unquestionably lose his
Shanghai gold bars, to say noth
ing of bis head.
THESE melancholy reflections
were not lightened by any
hope of escape; for. indeed, what
possibility of escape was there?
Even to get to the railway sta
tion. from which trains still de
parted fitfully for Peiping, it
would be necessary to penetrate
a cordon of Japanese machine
guns and bayonets. If he reach
ed the station, Ku was sure of
being recognized by the Japanese
sentries the moment he tried to
buy a ticket. And for that mat
ter, how could he even buy a
ticket? He had exactly two grimy
coppers on his person, and the
Japanese had offered five thou
sand dollars for Information
leading to his capture.
The General found himself in
volved In a stampede of his fel
low ricksha coolies. General Ku
raced madly with the rest, con
vinced that the foreign lady
might prove to be the key to his
obsessing riddle.
Ku's experience as a Manchur
ian war-lord and his education—
at Cornell University in the
United States of America—con
spired most helpfully in the race
for the ricksha fare. Two vet
eran coolies were ahead of him:
he delivered a Bhort but terrific
punch to the sagging kidneys of
one. (The General had been a
keen student of boxing at Cor
nell.) He tripped.up the other,
neatly enough. (The General had
been a rather brilliant inside
right on Cornell’s soccer team in
his day.)
The half-naked General and
his ricksha arrived only a stride
or two ahead of the*panting. gib
bering horde that surrounded
Miss Mary Waples. Still, he was
in time to say:
"Me sptakee Eng-aleesb vel
lee good, Missee, runnee chop
chop. Number One ricksha boy.”
Fortunately the General had
been a member of the dramatic
society also at Cornell, so he had
learned to speak English exactly
as Americans believed all Chinese
spoke the language.
Instinctive caution compelled
Miss Waples to scrutinize the
General most carefully before
she accepted his service. He was
certainly very ragged. But
among bis hairy, pock-marked,
snaggle-toothed, cross- eyed,
scrofulous colleagues. General
Hu's plump cheeks and big al
mond-shaped brown eyes In
spired trust. It was with only
a slight quaver that Miss Waples
commanded:
"Huang Ku-tun station, boy,
chop-chop. Baggage go truck.
I go ricksha.”
IT was lucky for General Ku
that Miss Waples had had but
flie most meager experience as
a ricksha passenger; otherwise
the General would have been
discharged before they had gone
twenty paces. He was thinking
industriously — a practice fatal
to ricksha coolies — about the
nossibility of utilizing the heav
en-sent Mis* Waples in his own
affairs. Running and thinking
both very seriously threatened
the dismemberment of Miss
Waples.
They were halted, duly scrut
inized. and passed by the trucu
lent Japanese sentry guarding
the single open highway between
the foreign quarter of Mukden
and the Chinese City, beyond
which lay their destination. Miss
Waples was almost as frightened
as General Ku—and disguised it
less than half as well — when
they finally w*ere allowed to roll
through the narrow passage be
tween barricades of barbed wire
mounted on sandbags.
Beyond this barrier the un
usual physical strain, plus a
cumulative anxiety, caused Gen
eral Ku to trot even more in
expertly than before. After a
bump that all but tossed Miss
Waples into the stew-pans of an
itinerant Chinese restaurateur,
she forgot her pidgin English
and cried out:
“Hey, what's the matter? Are
you ill?"
It was General Ku’s oppor
tunity. He staggered aud wheez
ed dreadfully. He dropped a
hand from the shaft of his rick
sha and pressed it to his heart.
“Heart sickee, vellee sickee,
Missee." he said, halting.
"Oh. you poor thing," said
Miss Waples automatically. Then
—for she was an extremely prac
tical person at times—she added:
"But you shouldn't be a ricksha
man if you have heart trouble."
"Oh, Missee, inside me all
light.” the bedraggled General
explained. "Soul sickee. this
fashion: me worree-worree my
wife. She my three piece baby
all four piece now b'long Pekin.
Me here, no can do go Pekin.
They no eatee all okay sure die."
THIS masterpiece of pidgin
both baffled and interested
Miss Waples. She asked ques
tions; General Ku answered them
glibly. There was a large, wet
tear on his cheek when he fin
ished his touching story. It
went like this:
He was a poor, hard-working
ricksha man. When the Japan
ese devils arrived, murdering
Chinese right and left—the lady
would see the bodies for herself,
in the streets—his only thought
was the salvation of his wife and
children. He took his five-years'
savings, rushed his family to
Huang Ku-tun, bought tickets
and sent them safely to Pekin
before the Japanese took charge
of the station and stopped the
exodus of Chinese. He supposed
his family to be safe, but only
safe to starve, because the Jap
anese no longer allowed Chinese
to leave Mukden, and his wife
and children had no money In
Pekin. Worse, he himself had
spent his last cent, and saw no
means of escape.
"Oh, the poor creature!" Miss
Waples was saying, half-aloud.
Miss Waples’ soft blue eyes,
clouding now with pity for her
unfortunate coolie, fell upon an
object in the gutter.
"Oh!" she said with a little
scream, half-rising from her seat
in the ricksha. "What’s that?
Oh, dear! Oh, dear!”
Ku Ta-inu smiled bitterly, but
withal sadly. He had well chosen
the spot for their halt.
"Chinese body." he remarked.
"Japanese catchee plenty Chin
ese dead body, leave ’em out
make look-see.”
Miss Waples’ blood froze with
horror. And this unhappy rick
sha man. with his poor little wife
in Pekin, and their three chil
dren,—-or was it five children?
—be, too, might be lying in the
street there in another day or
tw'o. cold and dead.
"Let’s go on a little." said Miss
Waples nervously. "Just a lit
tle. There, stop now — stop.
Can I do anything, you poor
man, to help you? I’ve com
pletely forgotten how to say any
thing in pidgin English now.
Listen—me help, if can do. What
can do help poor China boy?”
This effort, faltering though tt
w’as. appeared to be fully under
stood by the coolie.
"Missee catchee Wang go Pe
kin." he said Imploringly. ‘‘Can
do."
“You mean, take you to Pe
kin?” she asked. "No can do.
How?"
General Ku Ta-mu cursed the
necessity for talking in pidgin.
If be could have used his own
fluent and forcelul English. «he
Job would have been done in a
moment. Waving with one hand
toward a shop beside the road,
he said:
"Maybe catchee one piece
dead-box; Wang sleep all-same
dead man—maybe father."
Miss Waples. puzzled, follow
ed the direction of his pointing
hand. What she saw was a little
coffin-shop, the only place of
business in the street which re
mained open.
"I don’t understand." she
said. "You wantee go Pekin,
one piece coffin—oh. dear, what
a language! — one piece dead
man?”
"Missee gotchee father.” said
the General determinedly.
"S’pose father die. s’pose catchee
one piece dead-box go Pekin.
Wang all-same father. Missee
sabe?"
"Ah.” she said at last, shocked
into complete loss of her pidgin
English, "note I understand! You
want me to buy a coffin and put
you in it. and pretend that you’re
my father’s body, and that I’m
taking you to Pekin! Good
heavens!”
SHE looked wonderingly at the
resourceful cooM who could
By Vincent Sheean
evolve such a scheme. General
Ku nodded vigorously.
"Cutchee dead box," lie said,
“catchee dead box all okay go
Pekin-slde."
The bizarrrric of the proposal
stunned Miss Waples for quite
two minutes. Well, then. Miss
Waples thought with unprece
dented daring, why not? She
would not leave another human
being to be killed in the streets
If she could help it. She got down
out of her ricksha with a wholly
new air of decision.
“I think I’m probably insane."
she said to the coolie, "but I’m
going to do it. Or try it. at
least. Now how shall 1 say that
in pidgin? Let's see. . . Come
along, boy, chop-chop, buy dead
box.”
It is possible that no man
ever, bought his own coffin with
more eager delight than Ku
Ta-mu displayed in the next five
a
Miss Waples in her ricksha pursueil closely by ihc coffin hearer* couhl
scarce!) have e»c«|»ed the notice of an) Japanese sentry.
minutes. Miss Waples had scarce
ly finished speaking when he
hounded to the side of the road
and saluted the coffin-maker with
a sputter and plash of Chinese.
Miss Waples. clutching her hand
bag firmly, followed him. The
proprietor of the coffin-shop, a
stolid fat man, with a squeaky
voice, stood in the door of his
establishment and argued with
Ku Ta-mu automatically, loudly
and without pause for breath.
But suddenly a silence fell upon
them: they began to talk almost
in whispers, and disappeared at
last through the door into the
recesses of the shop. When her
ricksha coolie returned. Miss
Waples was beginning to get a
little nervous.
“Hundred yen,” said the
coolie. "Shop-man give box new
ricksha coolie catihee box go
Huang Ku-tun. Missee sabe?”
The only part of this recitative
which meant much to Miss
Waples was the first part. One
hundred yen did not seem an
excessive price for a fine, solid
looking coffin made of thick wood
in cylindrical shape, like a tree
trunk. varnished shinily all over.
“All right,*' she said, opening
her hand-bag. “I’ll give you
Japanese money—a hundred ven.
Here you are. But hurry up—
chop-chop, you sabe?"
"Chop-chop.” said the General,
retiring in delight with two fifty
yen notes. One of these he slip
ped neatly into the twisted belt
of his rapped cotton trousers;
the other he presented, with a
flourish, to the undertaker.
MISS WAPLE8 then witnessed
a succession of events which
seemed to her almost matter-of
fact. as if nothing else might be
expected in a place like Mukden.
Walking to the door, she peered
into the little shop. Her rick«ha
coolie, smiling solemnly, had
climbed into one of the cylinders
of wood and was being enclosed
there by the fat undertaker.
This process completed, the ur
dertaker ornamented the end of
the box with some carefully
painted hieroglyphics which, so
his meaning eye and constant
chuckles indicated, were intended
to deceive the Japanese. Cus
toms-marks or a military pass,
said Miss Waples wisely to her
self, and marveled at the ingenu
ity of the Oriental mind. Then
the undertaker clapped his hands
and shouted something. Two
young coolies appeared In re
sponse and, lifting the coffin
which contained Ku Ta-mu,
marched through the door into
the street. There they stood mo
tionless, waiting with their bur
den. Meanwhile the proprietor
of the shop, smiling ingratiating
ly at the foreign lady, motioned
her back into her ricksha. An
other shout—a louder one this
time—caused three or four be
draggled Chinese to materialize
out of the apparently deserted
houses on the street; one of
these whs appointed to pull Mias
Waplea’ ricksha.
The full impropriety of her
present enterprise presented it
self to her in every possible as
pect as they made their way to
the Huang Ku-tun station. How
could she journey all the way to
Pekin with a Chines* coolie in a
coffin? What would the Japan
ese soldiers say or do if they
chanced to open the box?
Miss Wap.es in her ricksha,
pursued closely by the coffin
bearers with the box containing
Ku Ta mu, could scarcely have
escaped the notice of any Japan
ese sentry. Nor did she; hut
fortunately there was a world of
honesty in Miss Waples' blue
eyed countenance. And the cus
toms-murks or military pass
which, Miss Waples believed, had
been painted on the end of the
coffin — those hieroglyphics ap
plied by the jocular undertaker
with a wink — seemed to stop
the Japanese sentries each time
just as they were about to open
the box. When her new ricksha
coolie and the coffin-bearers
identified her positively, at each
such crisis, as a foreign lady
with the body of her most noble
father, the Japanese sentries
asked no more.
Miss Waples, therefore, ar
rived safely and in good time at
the Huang Ku-tun station,
bought her ticket, and collected
her luggage from the hotel por
ter. In a paroxysm of dread—
not to be guessed from her firm
and dignified manner — she
watched the Japanese officer who
appeared to be in charge of the
station draw near.
"You are going to Pekin,
madam?’* he asked in correct
enough English. At the end of
the sentence he hissed courteous
ly. to indicate respect.
“Yes, sir.” said Miss Waples.
She plunged desperately on.
“»’m taking with me this —
this coffin." she said, "with the
remains of my late father, who
died on the way.’*
The young Japanese officer
bowed ceremoniously.
“Is tint so!” he said. This
cryptic phrase was followed by
several rapid clucking noises and
a prolonged hiss. Miss Waples
had been a whole week in Japan
and Manchuria—long enough to
learn that these sounds signified
condolence, politeness, and a
willingness to be of service.
“I have taken a whole com
partment,” she said, "so as to
be alone with—”
lere sLe paused, uncertain
what to call th object w'hich
traveled with her.
“I regret.” he salu. ”the death
>f your honored parent. I hope
that you will have h safe Journey
to Pekin. The men can pass.”
MISS WAPLES saw the coffin
with her late father’s re
mains safely through the sen
tries and deposed on the cush.
ions of a first-class compartment
in the Pekin train. Then — a
little late, perhaps — compunc
tion seized hex.
"Mary Waples,” she said
aloud, "you’re just a fool, just a
plain fool — and dishonest to
bool."
She had just reached this con
clusion when a jovial shout be
hind her put all further reflec
tion to rout.
“Well, here's Miss Waples!
Mother, here's Miss Waples!
Caroline, Miss Waples is here!
Now didn’t I tell you we’d see
Miss Waples on the train?”
Miss Waples turned arouftd as
if she had been poked with a
stick. “The Winterbothtmif”
she was thinking.
"Hello, Dr. Winterbotham,'*
she said. "How nice to see you!
1 thought you were goiug to stay
on for several more davs in
Mukden.”
"Our friends wanted us to
stay,” said Dr. Winterbotham
earnestly, “but Mother and I
couldn't bear the sight of all this
suffering. So we are going on
to Pekin at once. Where are
you? Is this your compartment?
Mercy! My dear Miss Waples,
is that — isn’t that — a Chinese
coffin?”
“Precisely,” she said. “A
Chinese coftin. I'll tell you about
it afterwards. Here’s Mrs. Win
terbotham — how are you, dear
Mrs. Winterbotham?”
Dr. Winterbotham had been
for fifteen years in the Orient.
“Whal'd I tell you, Mother?”
he demanded. “Here's Miss
Waples. and although she’s only
been off the boat a week she's
already got the collecting mania.
Look what she’s begun with —
coffins!”
Miss Waples, submitting to a
very dry kiss from Mrs. Winter
botham, said "Shf* quite dis
tinctly. The Doctor, alive to his
own dignity, went from Jolly to
earnest again in a second.
“Please. Dr. Winterbotham,
excuse me from talking about it
just now,” said Miss Waples.
"I'll tell you later this evening.
The train hasn’t even started
yet.”
“You will come and eat din
ner with ns, my dear,” said Mrs.
Winterbotham with an air of
finality. “They say there’s some
kind of restaurant-car on the
train in spite of all the trouble.
I must get back to poor dear
Caroline. She has a dreadful
cold.”
Miss Waples. murmured polite
sympathy and turned to go into
her own compa.tment. There
she sat down, plump! — and
stared at the box on the seat op.
posite her.
The Winterbothams' How in
the name of heaven could she
ever explain the existence of the
coolie in the coffin to Dr. and
Mrs. Winterbotham? The Win
terbothams had a way of expect
ing c.nd believi.tg the worst;
they had believed the worst of
everybody all the way across on
the boat.
Hut meanwhile the tram bad
started, and even as she sat
there, staring at the troublesome
wooden box, the lid of it began
to open slowly. She leaped to
her feet and put it down again.
“Bh!” she hissed at it. "Be
quiet! Oh. dear, how do you say
anything in pidgin? Chop-chop,
I say, chop-chop!”
She glanced nervously around.
There wert curtains at every
window. Including the ones on
the co.ridor. In a moment she
had drawn them all shutting the
compartment into almost total
darkness.
“New breathe, if you must,”
she said to the object on the
other seat, “but hurry up about
it: chop-chtp!”
"All okay, Misree,” said a
voice from the box a'ter a mo
ment, and she heart. the lid close
gently.
THE reflectfc ns of Miss Waples
during the hours btfrre din
ner were not of a cheerful na
ture. Nevertheless Miss Waples
marched firmly enough, just be
fore dinner-time, into the com
partment occupied by the Win
terbotham family.
"I’ve come to tell you about
that coffin.” she said. "It s a
man. That is. there’s a man in
it.”
Mrs. Winterbotham, a frost
bitten lady with plaintive small
eyes, moaned softly.
"1 kmtc there was something.”
she said with conviction.
' Let me handle this. Mother,”
said Dr. Winterbotham in his
Grade-A earnest manner. "We
understand, Miss Waples, that
you have given out that you are
traveling with the corpse of your
father, is that correct?”
"it Is." said Miss Waples.
"May I ask why?” the Doctor
proceeded.
"Because a man — that Is, a
ricksha coolie -whose wife and
children had escaped to Pekin,
wanted to join them there. It
was the only way to get him
through the Japanese lines."
"A rick*hu coolie’” said Dr.
Winterbotham,
"Oh!” said Mrs. Winter
botham. "Oh, Ulysses!”
"You are going to Pekin to
become the head of a girls'
school. Miss Waples.” said the
Doctor. “Do you intend to ar
rive there in the company of a
ricksha coolie?”
* 1 intend to get that man
through to Pekin if 1 tan. It’s
common humanity."
"I think we are better judges
of that than you are, Miss
Waples.’' said Dr. Winterbotham
loftily. "I tell you it is abso
lutely impossible for you—that
is. if you are telling us the whole
truth."
"That is enough, sir,” said
Miss Waples, getting up. "I
don’t tell lies.”
And as she spoke she thought
remorsefully of ’he little Japan
ese lieutenant, with his reverence
for even the foreigner* an
cestors.
"1 warn you,” the Doctor said
as she moved toward the door.
"I wa-n you that when this gets
known in Pekin it will be very
bad for you. And as for our
Caroline—”
"Indeed,” said Mrs. Winter
hotham. “our Caroline will not
be enrolled in your school, Miss
Waples.”
As this sentence rolled majes
tically out, little Caroline, a fat
girl with a bad cold, opened one
eye.
"Ho*, dog!” she remarked, but
nobody paiu any attention to her.
TllISS WAPLES was furious,
ivl if ai, ht had been needed
to strengthen her determination
the Winterbothams had now pro
vided it. But somewhere in the
undercurrent of her thoughts
there persisted a feeling that she
liad after ail been very foolish—
that the headmistress of a girls’
school had no right to such ad
venturous impulses as hers. She
ordered some food in her com
partment. and when she had fin
ished eating, she opened the cof
fin-lid and dropped a sandwich
in. There was no need to starve
the creature, she supposed. And
as the weary evening wore on.
with the coffin-lid ever more fre
quently raising itself an inch or
two to admit air. Miss Waples
gave herself over to the delights
of pessimism. She would lose
her job in Pekin as soon as she
arrived, she decided, and all be
cause a poor ragged ricksha
coolie was terrilied of the Jap
anese.
IN time Miss Waples went to
sleep. Turning off all the
lights except one above the cor
ridor door she lay down, fully
clothed, with a steamer rug over
her. She was asleep when the
coffin-lid opened and her rick
sha coolie General Ku. — every
bone in his luxurious body ach
ing—got out and stretched him
self. The General stood for a
long time Jooking down at Miss
Waples. When her steamer-rug
slipped and was in danger of fall
ing to the floor he replaced it
carefully. At last, as a tread
was heard down the corridor, the
General leaped back into his cof
fin. quick as a mouse, and pulled
the lid down again. He was
there, silent and quaking when
the last of the Japanese soldiers
poked his head through the door
and saluted in respect for the
foreign lady who was traveling
with the corpse of her most noble
parent.
Miss Waples slept late: she
made her way down the corridors
to the restaurant-car after a
rather sketchy toilette.
"Good morning.’’ she said as
she passed the Winterbothams*
table.
Mrs. Winterbotham murmured
something into her coffee-cup:
the Doctor, stern and sad. gazed
out the window Hut little Caro
line made the really distinguish
ed pronouncement of the occa
sion.
"Good morning. Miss Waples.”
said she. “How's your man?”
Hot with embarrassment, Miss
Waples made her way to a cor
ner and sat down. Besides the
missionary family and herself
there were no foreigners in the
carriage, and only three Chinese.
The zone of Japanese occupation
had been left behind hours be
fore; the train moved along slow
ly; the Chinese hoys in attend
ance seemed nervous, jumpy.
The reasons for this behavior
were al’. too soon apparent; for
in another quarter of an hour,
as the train sighed to a full stop,
a scattered burst of rifle-fire
saluted ■. 0ne or two of the
bullets struck the restaurant-car
low dowu. aud made a shocking
•latter.
"Uung-hutse, Miasee, hung
hulzt " said one of the boys to
Alias Waples. lie cowered be
hind her chair; the whole car
was stricken with terror.
Miss Waples sat quiet. She
knew what Auity-Autre meant—
the red beards, the Manchurian
bandits! Meanwhile it was odd
to hear Mrs. Winterboibaoi
screaming, to see the Doctor ply
her with water. They were all
silent enough In a moment, when
the door at the end of the car
filled with armed figures,
Chinese, these—tall, slovenly,
sullen, with rifles and colorless
dirty uniforms.
The man in charge of the
group at the door motioned with
his rifle, muttered a command or
two.
“We’ve got to go.” said the
Doctor. “Come on. Miss Waples.
Be quiet, Caroline. Come along.
Mother. Don't cry. It's no use."
rpiIK four of them filed out the
door and down the car steps
to a railroad platform. At one
side of them was a water-tank;
there were three or four huts
to be seen, and beyond that noth
ing but the flat Manchurian
plain. Two riflemen stood before
them and two behind.
“What will they do with us?"
Miss Waples asked.
"Take us to their camp. 1 sup
pose. wherever that la." said the
Doctor. "They look like ex
soldiers; a little better than or
dinary A Map-Autre, anyway. Be
quiet. Caroline.”
One of the riflemen prodded
the Doctor slightly and delivered
a command.
"We re to go to a house.” said
the Doctor. ”1 imagine one of
these huts. Filthy holes.”
”Oh, 1 can t go.” said Miss
Waples suddenly. “What about
that poor coolie in the coffin?
What will they do to him?”
“Kill him. probably.” said the
Doctor with a certain air of ap
proval. “He's no good to them.
They’ll get money out of us. and
out of the rich Chinese, if there
are any on the train; they don’t
bother with coolies.”
“Oh. I must do something.”
said Miss Waples In deep dis
tress. “I brought the poor crea
ture here. Speak to them."
“Ouch!” said the Doctor. "I
can’t talk with this fellow pok
ing me! Come along now; we
can talk later.”
Surrounded by their guard of
riflemen they made their way
across the field to one of the
huts alongside the tracks. And
here, on a kind of mud dais built
against the wall, all four of them
sat for a full hour, uncomfort
able and hopeless, while their
guards stood in the open door.
They were not entirely without
hints that something was going
on, for they heard cheers on two
occasions.
Another time there was evi
dence of excitement among their
guards, who chattered volubly,
made gestures, laughed.
But what finally happened was
to nobody more surprising than
to Miss Waples. She was won
dering for the thousandth time
what had become of the remains
of her late honored parent, when
the guards at the door presented
arms, there was a murmur of
voices, and General Ku Ta-mu,
transformed by a borrowed uni
form. stood in the doorway.
“You can go back to your
places on the train now.” he
said “There was a — a little
misunderstanding, but it is all
straightened out now. The train
w-ill proceed to Pekin; your bag
gage is intact. I am sorry that
you have been inconvenienced.”
Dr. Winterbotham recognized
the war-lord at a glance.
“Misunderstanding, sir?" he
asked truculently, rising to his
feet. “Marshal Ku Ta-mu, 1
shall complain to the American
Legation of this treatment. It
is an outrage. My wife and child
and this lady have been sitting
In this hut for an hour. One of
Your Excellency’s rascally sol
diers poked me twice with his
rifle. We were interrupted in
the midst of our breakfast. And
you call it a misunderstanding"’
The Marshal smiled, and his
brown eyes — sad no longer —
wandered toward the astounded
countenance or Miss Waples. “If
these men had been Uunfi-hutze,
Dr. Winterbothara.” he inquired!
“wouldn’t you have had worse
things to complain of? As a
matter of fact I believe their in
tentions were—well—fairly seri
ous when they stopped the train.
Until they found me lying in a
coffin in your friend’s compart
ment. that is. I was able to per
suade them otherwise.”
“Bandits!” said the Doctor,
fuming. "They're all bandits!”
No surprise was going to di
vert him from the grievance he
now held against the Chinese
government, which — at a con
servative though rapid estimate
— he judged might bring him
about fifteen thousand dollars
In gold when the diplomats put
the claim through
“Take your places in the
train," said the war-lord shortly,
“and thank your friend Miss
Waple# that you are able to do
ao She may tell you why.”
Nowadays, of course. Miss
Waples Is frequently obliged to
tell the story of her extraordin
ary journey across Manchuria,
for she is a heroine to all Peip
ing, including the Winterboth
ams. She is a great deal more
knowing about it now than she
was at the time.
“Of course I can't say that I
recognized him in his disguise.”
she says romantically but honest
ly. “And still, there was a <
marked difference—he wasn't in ,
the least like the other cooliea—
and one can tell such things at
a glance, don’t you think?”

Scenes and Persons in the Current News
I—Theodore Roosevelt Memorial hall of the American Museum of Natural History in New York which
was dedicated by President Roosevelt. 2—Wreckage of the big air liner which fell in an Arkansas swamp,
carrying 17 persons to their death. 3—Col. Edgar S. Gorrell, who was elected “czar” of commercial aviation
by the newly organized Air Transport Association of America.
Mass Marriage of Chinese in Shanghai
Now the Chinese have taken the mass marriage idea from the Occident. A parade of brides and their
bridegrooms into the temple grounds for the marriage ritual at a recent multiple wedding in Shanghai is
pictured. There were 130 couples knotted at the ceremony, and they are seen here, -brides in one line and
grooms in another, in the wedding march. o
CUBA’S NEW CHIEF
Dr. Miguel Mariano Gomez, for
mer mayor of Havana, was elected
president of Cuba.
Colors for Millinery Are
Copied From Ancient Urns
Colors from pottery made by
men who died 2,000 years ago will
adorn the hat of fashionable Miss
1936.
R. F. Wilson, of the British Col
or council, has been working at the
museums in South Kensington,
adopting subtle shades from Chi
nese vases, many of them dating
back to 200 B. C.
Here are some of the colors taken
from the pottery of ancient China:
Coolie, an unusual type of Saxe
blue which the coolie of China dyes
his clothes with to this day.
. Corn stalk, a golden yellow with
which the Chinese loved to deco
rate their beautiful vases.
Mandarin blue, made specially in
Yorkshire for export to China.
Ruby, the red of the precious
stone, taken from Chinese vases.
Lotus bud, a delicate pink, the
gsame color as the flower, -
WHITE BLUFFS SPOKESMAN
MISS PARIS, 1936
Mlle. Madelaine Balestre is here
flashing her beautiful smile imme
diately after she was declared “Miss
Paris of 1986” during a beauty con
test at the Moulin Rouge, Paris.
Pigeons of Trafalgar Square
These pige‘ons were apparently trying to find the “dicky bird” in the
camera when the photographer appeared in Trafalgar square in London.
Nothing Doing at Italian Border
The result of the sanctions against Italy has been that practically
all traffic over the borders has been suspended. This photograph shows
the closed gates of the Italian-Swiss border at Chiasso, where once there
was heavy traffic in goods. -
Test Robot in Lightship
Off Coast of Michigan
Detroit, Mich.—The United States
lighthouse service is experimenting
near here with a new type of robot
warning ship that may replace many
present-day lighthouses.
Eight miles off the Michigan
coast in Lake St. Clair—connecting
lake between Lakes Erie and Huron
—a crewless ship performs all the
functions of warning mariners of
nearby danger. g
As soon as darkness closes down
a warning beacon flares, a radio
beacon sends steady calls and fog
signals blare warnings during
murky weather., In a lighthouse
along the coastline the keeper mere
ly presses buttons to work the ap
paratus.
The present ship, in operation for
the last six months, will continue
as an experiment for another year.
The success of its operation prob
ably will decide the future policy
of the government service to sea
farers.
WOMAN MAYOR
Mrs. Barbara Hanley, of Webb
wood, in northern Ontario, twenty
years ago a school teacher in the
town, now has the distinction o!f
being Canada’s first woman mayor,

We have learned something
this year about Christmas tree
gathering.
A year ago, a week ahead of
Christmas, we went out to the
flats at the end of Brooklyn to
get our own tree, as is the cus
tom. It was a beautiful clear
day, with snow on the ground.
We started at this end of the
flats, searching for a proper speci
men. Exactly 3 hours later, after
sizing up every shrub and growth
in the neighborhood over an area
of 13 acres we went back to the
\ first one, cut it down, and took
it home. We still thought we
could have done better if it had
n’t got dark so soon.
This year, on Sunday, the day
scheduled for tree getting, the
rain was coming down in torrents
all day. And torrents is putting
it mildly. But having to stick to
our schedule, we sallied forth
with our keyhole saw.
We hastened straight out
Brooklyn, walked 8 feet off the
road, asked how about this one
of the first tree we came to, got
approval, sawed it down, and
hurried home with it. We were
soaking wet and so was the tree,
but the round trip took exactly
23 minutes.
The tree is just, the right
height, the branches are bounti
ful and there are no bare spaces
in it.
As we Bay, we have ©aine
something about Christmas-tree
gathering.

recently in Tangku, China, and
from there wrote to his old Kod
iak boss, Harvey Edwards, the
painter, as follows:
”Taku Garrison, our Dew
camp was built by the Japanese.
Permanent brick buildings had
been erected with coolie labor.
Now the Japanese have moved
out and we have taken advant
i age of their conveniences. A
brick wall with an electric guard
fence surrounds the compound.
Although Japanese living stand
ards and methods of construction
do not compare with the Ameri
can, yet it was good to move in
to substantial buildings after
what we had in the Philippines.
’’Since coolie labor is so cheap
we use many Chinese in our
camp. A large.number of our men
are busy unloading ships in the
harbor.
’’Tangku is interesting but un
attractive. Tientsin, 30 miles a
way, has large modern buildings,
industry, paved streets, crowded
multitudes, interesting shops and
is a progressive city. The Ameri
can dollar is worth about 15000
in Chinese dollars.

arriving and winter promiets to
be cold. We will have some kind

from Asia.
The Trade in Coolies?Eow and

Character, Habits and Economics
of the Chinese.
The question of Chinese emigration to the United
States ta at present attracting a great <lcal or public
attention, and man; of the leading men of the south,
Bouth went and even the Northwest have earnestly
taken up tne subject, and are applying to all known
sources Irom which to derive Information. The
public press has thrown but little light on the question,
for the reason that It has had no means of obtaining
accurate and reliable accounts or this vast
hiwinAHu anil nnr utar/>4ii\rn mwl h'nrlnicr men
perfectly ignorant or wltut jhey commonly call the
coolte trade. Even Mr. Koopmanschaiip, wUo assumes
10 be an oracle on this subject, is unacquainted
wiili it further than in receiving Chinese
people in California, for he lias never visited China
In Ins life. The opposition to this coolie movement
arises from this general state of ignorance in
regain to it, ana it was through this
Ignorance, together, It must be admitted,
with a good deal of fanaticism, that the
act of Congress of January, imjj, was
passed -to prohibit the coolie trade." Many of the
leading men in the New England States protest
against the importation of coo ties to this country
because they believe the coolie trade and the slave
trade are identical; and even were they convinced
that the coolie Is simply an emigrant, who comes to
make fruitful the barren wastes throughout the
length and breadth of the South, the Southwest and
the West; that he comes (o build our railroads and
canals, and even U> fill our workshops and factories
and make tnem busy with his industry, st ill the New
fcnglanders would oppose him, for they are opposed
to everybody and everything that is likely to conflict
with their interests. That this coolie emigration enterprise
will ultimately be perfectly successful no
one can doubt after being enlightened on the subject.
The next ten years will give us an enormous
Chinese population, particularly in the Sonth; and
In view of this fact the purpose of this article Is simply
to give an exhaustive, comprehensive and
impartial history of the coolie trade in all its
ram ill cations.
Nearly all nations have been, and are now, dealing
Id tne cooue business, ami every nation that has
permitted these people to labor (or it luis become
winched thereby. England, France, Spain, Holland,
Portugal and 1'ern have long Biuce acknowledged
the benefits they have derived from Chinese labor.
The largest business that was done for some years
was m the Cuban trade, and a number or first clans
bouses in Havana were engaged In it, such as Drake
A Co., Job6 Maria Morales, Campbell ?fc Co., Torrlces,
Puentf A Co., Ferran A Dupierrls, Ac. About the
year 1868 the Spanish government put some impediments
In the way of thus frame, and it roll off a Utile
lor a year or two, but afterwards begun aualn with
fresh vigor. Hie business until 1864 had been principally
carried on by merchants and joint stock
companion of Havana, but in that year some of the
planters began to receive coolies on their own account.
Don Miguel Aluama sent an agent to Macao
in 1864, and until 1807 received large shipments.
Otoer planters followed Ills example, but only on a
mall scale. The leading houses before the revolution
in Cuba were the Alianza Company and Messrs.
Lombiilo, Montalvo A Co. Tlic French Uonse or
t'aro, norms A Watson recalled their nseut last year,
as did also all the other Havana houses.
COST AM) PKOFIT.
Dp to the year 1853 the cost of landiu# a coolie in
Cuba was aoout $120, Mexican. Of course there
were expenses after the landing to be added to this,
but at that time the lirolceratre in China was Nplilmn
over live dollars per head, while in 1857 it was
cighty-flve per coolie. Coolie contracts have been
Hold us high as twenty-six ounces ($44;!) and as low as
twelve ounces. Tlic importers make a great manv
bad debts, and from this cause and also from the
occasional great number ol deaths on shipboard
their profits arc not so large as they otherwise would
be. In 1866 and 1807 coolie contracts were disposed
or on as Ions credits as two years und without interest.
The most experienced coolic trader is an
American, Captain Francis Bowen, a native of New
York, who lius for the last sixteen years been engaged
In the coolie business in China. He formerly
commanded a numuer of coolie ships, but for years
past he has resided in Macao, where he lias been the
agent for the shipment of coolies to the Spanish
West Indies. He is thoroughly acquainted with the
Chinese laws and customs, and with international
laws governing the coolie trade, and has been very
successful. Probably there is no person engaged In
the trade who has a greater experience than Uaptaiu
Bowen, and ho certainly is more extensively known
than others lu the busluess.
SUPPLY AND DEMAND?TIIR PERUVIAN COOI.IK
TRADE.
The Peruvian coolie trade has, without doubt,
been the causo of tne prejudice which most people
reel against this business, especially ttiose who do
not nuderstand how It is carried on. That grout
cruelty uud injustice have been practised towards
coolies in the Chlncha Islands cannot be contradicted.

and universality of the term is the permanence and constancy of the
spelling. Wherever the term has gone, the original spelling in very
large degree persists. In Samoa, where the influence of the phonctid
spelling of words which has obtained such a strong foothold wherever
the Polynesian was the indigenous language, seems especially strong,
the phonetic spelling "Kuli" is sometimes used.' But even there the
other and usual spelling seems to dominate.
Among most of those who use the term there is little thought given
cither as to its dcriviation, its origin, or its exact significance. Among
those who do give thought to the matter there seems a difference of
opinion. To some it means specifically a Chinese laborer, generically,
an Asiatic laborer, figuratively, an ill-paid laborer. Added to all this
there is sometimes the idea of a laborer of one of these various classes,
.who is laboring in a land alien to that of his birth.
Others hold that strictly the term Coolie, at least, in its origin meant
a laborer, generally a Chinese laborer, more loosely an Asiatic laborer,
who had been taken from his native country to labor elsewhere, either
by fraud or force. This idea probably arises from the fact that half a
century ago, when the term first began to make its way into European
languages the coolie was kidnapped in Hongkong, Macao or Canton,
crowded on shipboard with hundreds of his fellows and herded in bar
racoons like 'the African slave of an earlier time.
The' word "coolie" means "laborer" in the dialect of India, from
which it came priginally. The Chinese say they derived it from "ku li,"
in their own tongue, meaning "bitter strength." That would have been
a fitting derivation, at any rate, for the coolie of the far cast works
hard for little money and eats the bread of bitterness.
This humble toiler is coming to be of more and more importance in
the world's' affairs as the years pass. He makes merchandise of his
strength in foreign lands as well as at home. All employers of labor
who know him praise the goods upon his shelves. "I sell my strength"
("Mai li chi") says the Chinaman. He lifts load. He digs in mines.
He draws burdens. The Russian in Manchuria and Siberia, the Eng
lishman in the Straits Settlements
Philippines these know his merits
arc 45,000 Chinese coolies digging
A fleet of steamships now is carrying coolies from Hongkong, Taku,
Weihaiwei and Sliinwangtao to Durban, South Africa, whence they are
hurried by rail, under strict guard, to the Transvaal. Impressions o
their thumbs are taken so they may be identified if they run away. They
wear numbered tags, are lodged in compounds and arc supplied with
food brought from China and sold to them at Taku prices. The first
consignment of these imported workers reached Durban June 18, 1904.
For a year the ships have been arriving and many more thousands are
to come.
Much satisfaction is expressed by the mine owners at the work done
by these coolie miners

fT^" 5' recent predominance of the plays
of William Shakespeare on the
.1. American stage has resulted in a
decided awakening of interest in
individual's personality, in eonse
e of which I have latterly been In
Ipt pf a large number of letters —
ih.iv were 923 of these In this morning's
mail alone—asking me to devote one In
stallment of this series of Father Time's
Recollections to Shakespeare alone.
After much discussion pro and con as to
propriety of yielding to this request, it
has be«n decided by my superiors—that
is, 6y those whom an accident of birth
has placed above me officially—that no
harm can come of my doing so, provided
it is definitely announced at the beginning
that we are not establishing a precedent
by which we shall be governed later in
the face of similar requests.
Shakespeare, after all, still enjoys a
splendid isolation in the field of dramatic
, lure, and. though we give up to him
nil of the space at our command this
morning, that is no reason why the fol
lowers of Mr. Clyde Fitch, or the admir
-6t Mr. Augustus Thomas, or the
supporters of Mr. Sydney Rosenfeld
should expect a similar acquiescence were
they to make a similar demand. With
this uliiTi IHi UHlliift. clearly presented as
above, we now proceed to the Shake
spearian Reminiscences of the Bard of
Avon—Editor.
B B B
The Boyhood of Shakespeare
MY earliest recollection of the man
who subsequently became famous
as the Bard of Avon dates back to
his boyhood, verging, I might almost say.
upon his infancy. He was five years of
age when I first happened upon him play
ing in the garden at the rear of hia fa
ther's dwelling at Stratford. Even then
there were faint foreshadowings of his
m mendous interest in the theater, as the
following incident goes far to prove.
He was standing before the open door
of the family hencoop, and whenever any
one of his companions endeavored to pass
| Some Horrors of the Coolie System.
HP HE mine owners of South Africa,
_l whose poorly paid Kaffir labor has
forsaken them since the Boer war,
nre turning to the coolie system as the sal
vation of their industry. While the sug
ion to return to this nineteenth cen
tury substitute for slave labor haa been
declared by them to be the only means of
reviving the gold mining industry of the
Hand. King Edward has intervened re
cently in .such a manner that the project
of introducing Chinese labor under the,
old conditions of the coolie system will
not be undertaken immediately, at least.
The Inuinn coolies who have been Im
ported have, according to a recent dis
patch; already introduced the bubonic
plague into the Rand.
There is talk also in our Southern
states of the advantage which would ac

In the middle of the last century have

days when the trade was at its height re
garding the practices attending the trans
portation of these unfortunates from their
native land to the scenes of their future
labors that reeked with' the odors of the
earlier African slave traffic. The coolies
in some of the colonies where the coolie
system was adopted were little better
off than had been the slaves whom they
succeeded.
The coolie system of the middle of the
last century was due to the emancipation
of all slaves throughout the British em
pire. In the tropical colonies the slaves
had been employed on the sugar planta
tions. When the slaves were released,
the planters of a number of the West In
dian islands, Demerara, Mauritius and
other sugar producing countries, looked
about to tind a class- of labor to succeed
them, as the negroes by nature were lzy,
and when they could not be compelled to
work, they declined, just a? the Kaffirs
have done, to do more work than they
chose to do. They could not be depend
ed upon. As white labor could not be
satisfactorily used in the tropics, the
planters hit upon the idea of importing
large quantities of labor from the over
jHipuluted -Asiatic countries—lndia and
China. Before the system was finally
stopped; hundreds of thousands of poor
Asiatics had been transferred from their
native land to foreign lands, never to re
turn. A large proportion of them were
inveigled away by the promise of wages
which they were never able to earn or
secure. They were, therefore, never able
to return t() their homes in the comfort
able circumstances they had dreamed of,
and were thus forever separated from
their families. Many were actually kid
niiped m the same way as negro slaves
had previously been taken. Countries
Which did not show the same care for the
coolie that Great Britain displayed were
willing: to wink at abuses which coolie
traders, or "pig stoalers." as they were
familiarly called, permitted. Public offi
cials were bribt-d to see nothing, and the
kidnapers became so bold that they would
swoop down on embassies going from one
.Asiatic country to another and carry off
the members to make coolie laborers of
them.
The lot of the coolies who were taken
from India under the care of the Indian
government was immeasurably better
than that of those who fell into the
hands of the Spanish or Portuguese. The
worst that could be said of the lot of
th< se was that they were deceived as
U> the amount of wages which they would
be able to earn when they reached their
dt-stination. But schoolmasters, priests,
weavers, scribes, professors, gamblers,
beggars and others totally unfit for the
labor to be performed were taken, they

Shakespeare's Anachronisms
SHAKESPEARE'S days In London
were by no means days of ease and
comfort. The vary brilliance of
the age in which he lived was such as to
compel him to live Intellectually at all
times at high pressure. From the queen
herself down to the humblest occupant
of a seat in the pit, every one was a
critic, and the mere production of a new
work waa an Invitation to the public to
come in and tear him and it to pieces.
In the theater and out of it Shakespeare
found himself liable at any moment to be
put upon the defensive, and I must say
that of all the men it has been my good
fortune to meet, from Adam to Andrew
Carnegie, none has ever shown a greater
preparedness to meet any kind of an on
slaught. A case of this was developed
one evening at the Green Room club in
London, shortly af^er the production of
that wonderful tragedy of "Coriolanus."
Raleigh was the bright particular baiter
of the evening, and he chose to take ex
ceptions to the anachronisms of that play.
••You talk of Cato, who was three or
four centuries later than Coriolanus," said
he, "and of Alexander, who didn't appear
for quite a hundred and -fifty years after
the period of your hero."
"I know that." said Shakespeare, "and
you have written live three-column let
ters to the Times on the subject, haven't
you?"
receiving the impression that they were
to have an opportunity to practice their
vocations in the new land. They did not
always receive justice in the courts when
it came to the adjudication of a case
of a coolie against a planter, and the
coolie's social necessities were not al
ways thought of. The condition of the
coolie on the British sugar plantation has
been described as follows: "Take a large
factory in Manchester, or Birmingham, or
Belfast, build a wall around it, shut in
itg working people from all intercourse,
save at rare intervals, with the outside
would, keep them in absolute heathen
ignorance, and get all the work you can
out of them; treat them not unkindly,
leave their social habits and relations to
themselves as matters not concerning you
who make the money from their labor,
and you would have constituted a little
community resembling, in no small de
gree, a sugar estate in British Guiana."
According to ihe stories told of the
trials of the cdeffea taken from China to
Cuba and Peru, the situation of the coolie
in a British colony was a bed of roses by
comparison. Shipload after shipload of
Chinese coolies were sent to Cuba and
Peru, bound to serve the planters far
periods of seven or eight years. Many of
these were kidnaped or induced to en
gage under false pretenses. Tales of
poorly equipped and badl~ overcrowded
transport ships were reported. It was
said that on th'-ir voyages all the horrors
of the 'Middle Passage" in the African
slave ships when the hatches were bat
tened down in torrid heat, were repro
duced. Those who reached Peru alive
were put up at auction as if they were
slaves. The only difference between their
condition and that of negro slaves was
that their slavery was limited to a period
of years. Contrary to agreement, many
were sent to work in the foul guano pits
on the Chincha islands off Peru instead of
being employed on the- plantations. Here
they were forced to labor in gangs, each
gang under an overseer arm»'d with a
cowhide lash rive feet long and one and
a half inches in thickness. When called
to account for this kind of treatment the
overseers attempted to show their merci
fulness by the excuse that the Weapon
"was not much used" before 4 o'clock.
After that hour the flagging energies of
the weaker oooHm were refreshed by cuts
of the whip. Remonstrances and entreaty
were met "by a flogging little short of
murder." This horrible condition was
brought to the attention of the British
government by- shipmasters engaged in
the guano trade. United States skipper*
declared that in all their knowledge of
slave trade they knew of nothing ap
proaching that of the middle island of

This drove the Peruvian trade in coolies

There the Peruvian and Cuban "labor

their return from trips for the purpose
along the Chinese coast. Many were
bought from the leaders of turbulent fac

course of their petty wars with each
other. Macao became known as a "coolie
