It is generally believed that Bacon
signed Shakespeare's name to 'Tlamlet"
out of sheer revenge for the trick the lat
ter had put upon him.
« O «
The Authorship of Hamlet
\PROPOS of the authorship of "Ham
lef' there waa a deal of discussion
during Shakespeare's own time
upon that much mooted point. There
were some who went so far as to say that
it was written .by Queen Elizabeth herself,
-nil that interesting lady when taxed with
it would shake her head roguishly and sig
nificantly remark that the ax was might
ier than the pen, which her interlocutors
took to be an intimation that the subject
was one she did not care to discuss.
Of course, the name of' Bacon was
bandied about rather freely In connection
with the play, and the Daily Ma if offered
a reward of £1.000 to any one who would
lind the name of the author concealed
in a tale of love and adventure published
In its storiette column. Sir Walter Ra
leigh would never deny that he had had
a hand, in the production; nor. indeed,
would anybody else, not even Shakespeare
himself. The attitude of the reputed au
thor was extremely interesting to me al
ways, because it was so non-commital.
Such replies as he could be induced to
make to inquiries were worthy of the
01 acle.
One of these in particular occurs to me
as 1 write. It was made at a jrarden
party at Hampton Court, given by Queen
Elizabeth in honor of the* Earl of Leices
ter. A group of young people were sit
ting underneath the spreading branches
of a large oak not far from the Maze
colony/ and the agents who secured the
coolies were called "pig-stealers," "pig
dealers" and "man-stealers."
The capture by these pirates "of two
junks containing a number of men from
Annam, members and followers of an
embassy then on its way to the court of
the emperor of China at Peking with a
sum of money amounting to about $300,005
which they were conveying to the emperor
as tribute, resulted In a peculiar libel
suit against the publisher of an English
paper published at Hongkong. The pub
lishers had not minced matters in declaring
that the members of the embassy had been
sold into slavery, and after being impris
oned at Macao were shipped over seas
to Cuba and elsewhere to be swallowed
up with those who had gone before. He
had mentioned the governor of Macao
and a bribery fund in connection with
the incident. The Portuguese government
was supposed to be interested in the pros
ecution of the case. After making a stir
in Eastern circles it was decided in favor
of the publisher.
The iniquities of the Macao ' coolie
traffic were revealed in the trial at Hong
kong in 1872 of Kwok-a-Sing. a China
man, on a charge of mutiny. He had been
an emigrant on the French Ship Nou
vclle Penelope, which sailed from Maeuo
in October, 1871. with 300 coolies on
board. The coolies mutinied and lulled
the captain and seven of the crow. In
the course of the trial it appeared that
while some of the mutineers were hard
ened criminals, who had engaged in order
to get possession of the ship and plunder
it, at least one-third of the emigrants
on board had been kidnaped. The number
shipped from Macao in the course of a
year was sometimes as high as 10.000.
The trade was only destroyed in 1872,
when Great Britain and China began to
prohibit the fitting out of any vessel sus
pected of being engaged hi this trade in
any Chinese or British port. At the close
of 1873 the Portuguese government offi
cially declared the "exportation" of
coolies illegal, and the traffic which had
forcibly separated thousands from their
families and native land forever and con
demned them to the life of a slave was
at ;in end.
Chines.' cocllea are still employed in
the Straits Settlements, but under con
ditions equitable to the coolie. At the
end of the period for which they have
been employed they are returned home.
This return home has a picturesque and
humorous side to it. Naturally the cool
ies do not have staterooms, but are ex
pected to crowd together, j n view of the
small rate paid for their passage. As
the weather is warm, they prefer to spend
their time on deck, sleeping as well as
Spending the daylight hours there. Usu
ally there is not space for all on the deck,
ko not all can enjoy this mitigating fea
ture of-the voyage.

when Sliakespeare was brought into the

"Now, Mr. Shakespeare, do tell us who

"In that case, madam." said Shake
speare, gallantly. 'I shall never tell, for
thpii i shall be with you forever."
Of coarse the young person blushed, but
still held on to her point with a pertinacity
worthy of a better cause.
"Yon must tell us," said she. pouting.
"Well, I'll compromise," said Shake
speare. smiling. "I'll tell you who did not
write it."
"That's something, anyhow." observed
the young woman. 'Who did not write
it?"
"David Belasco." said Shakespeare, and
then, a second thought apparently flash-
Ing across his mind. hf> added quickly,
"That is. he hasn't yet.'-'

Ten Tips for Long Life.
First- Rise early, retire early and fill
your day with work.
Second —Water and bread maintain life;
pure air and sunshine are indispensable
to health; and do not worry. -
Third—Frugality and sobriety form the
best elixir of longevity.
Fourth—Cleanliness prevents rust: the
best-eared-for machines last the longest.
Fifth — Enough sleep repairs waste and
strengthens; too much sleep softens and
enfeebles.
Sixth—To be sensibly dressed is to give
freedom to one's movements ami enough
warmth to be protected from sudden
changes of temperature.
Seventh--A clean and cheerful house
makes a happy home.
Hisrhth- The mind is refreshed and in
vigorated by distractions and amusements.
but abuse of them leads to dissipation and
dissipation to vice.
Ninth —Cheerfulness makes love of life,
and love of life Is naif of health. On
the contrary, sadness and discouragement
hasten old age.

His Last Words
THE last words of Shakespeare arm
variously recorded. Some have it
That the parting words of
'Let fall the curtain, the play is done,"'
were spoken by Shakespeare. Others
have it that as the last moment approach
ed he was heard to- murmur: '-Just o';»
more round, boys. It's my shuffle." But
none of these is correct. The actual final
words oi" the great bard were far more af
fecting than either of these, and seemed
by me as they fell upon my ears to in
dicate an inward foreboding for the fu
ture which I deeply regretted at the time.
It was quite evident that the last min
ute was at hand, and I paused jS i
moment to make it si-em the lor
the sake of those about him, when ih»
poet opened his eyes and gazed ftx-.dly
at me.
"Is there anything I can do for you,
■William?" I whispered in his ear.
"Yes, C'hronos." said he faintly.
"What is ft—stay my hand a while?"
I asked.
"Oh. no," said he. "I <Vo not care to liv«.
on forever* only I woutd like an a«sbegtM

Why the Left Shoe Wears Out Before th«

will see- that invariably customers will
pttt out their right fog! when going to be

goitog to sit down over there."

took a seat, lifted her curtain of black
veiling and as the clerk approached

an expanse of skirt.
-It's always the case and I don t be-

manufacturers evidently are wise to this
fact as In the rarlons the right shoe la
always packed on top. Once I had a lot

the muscles to a greater extent. On tn«
other hand, the left foot is larger than
the right In most persons. The difterenee
is so slight that we seldom have trouble
In littinsr shoes, however. It Is the lett
shoe that wears out before the rfght, and
probably for this reason."—Shoe Retailer.

"Oh, Mr. Flipperley," she exclaim
ed, soulfully, '"have you ever felt a
dim, uneasy sense of oppression as
If the mere weight of life were a bur
den too heavy to be borne by the
chained spirit panting with psychic

"I invariably have sucn a feeling
at dinner time," was the callous re
sponse; "hut hitherto I have attrib
uted it to pickles."
